critic_raw:
The Memory Module's core claim — that it 'reduces representation capacity' to degrade anomaly reconstruction — conflates two distinct mechanisms that are not jointly validated. The PoC assumes that restricting the memory to prototypical normal patterns will naturally increase reconstruction error on anomalies, but this is only true if the memory is large enough to compactly represent all normal variation yet small enough to fail on anomalies. The memory size hyperparameter is therefore a critical free parameter that is neither theoretically motivated nor empirically swept across diverse anomaly types. There is no ablation showing what happens as memory size varies from pathologically small (where normal data itself reconstructs poorly) to large (where the module effectively memorizes anomalies too, since test-time update allows memory drift).

The Test-Time Updation strategy introduces a fundamental evaluation contamination risk. Updating memory items during inference on a test set means the model's behavior on sample T depends on all samples seen before T. This ordering dependence invalidates the standard i.i.d. assumption underlying AUC-ROC computation: the reported AUC is not a statistic over independently evaluated samples but over an adaptive process. The order in which normal and anomaly test frames are presented will affect which memory slots get updated and when, creating evaluation instability that is never characterized.

The hybrid Abnormality Score combining PSNR and L2 distance between queries and memory items has a dimensionality mismatch problem that is unaddressed. PSNR operates in pixel space (input reconstruction quality) while L2-to-memory operates in feature space (encoder output). These two signals are not on commensurate scales and their combination weight is a free hyperparameter. The paper presents one setting without sensitivity analysis, so the reported results may reflect a tuned combination that does not generalize to other datasets or anomaly types.

The Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss are designed to push memory items apart while clustering normal features toward them. This creates a tension: if memory items are too spread out (separateness too strong), each item covers a narrow slice of normal variation, increasing false positives on normal edge cases. If too compact, items collapse and the module degenerates to a single prototype. No phase diagram or sensitivity study is provided for these competing loss weights, so the reported performance likely reflects a locally good hyperparameter setting rather than a robustly learnable configuration.
defender_raw:
The Memory Module design is well-grounded in a principled motivation: by constraining the CNN to reconstruct inputs through a finite set of memory prototypes drawn from normal training data, the system creates an asymmetry that is genuinely useful for anomaly detection. Normal inputs should find nearby prototypes in memory and reconstruct cleanly; anomalous inputs cannot map to any stored prototype faithfully, causing degraded reconstruction. This is not an ad-hoc trick — it directly operationalizes the assumption that normal data lies on a compact manifold that can be discretized.

The choice of a hybrid Abnormality Score combining PSNR and L2 distance between queries and memory items is also defensible. PSNR captures pixel-level reconstruction quality on the output side, while the L2 distance in the memory query space captures how far the input representation is from any stored normal prototype. These two signals are complementary: an anomalous input can sometimes produce a plausible-looking reconstruction if the decoder is powerful enough, but its query distance will still be large. Using both hedges against each failure mode individually.

The Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss address a genuine failure mode of vanilla memory modules: without explicit regularization, memory items may collapse to a few dominant prototypes, reducing coverage of the normal manifold. Separateness encourages memory items to be spread across the space of normal patterns, while Compactness encourages query representations to cluster tightly around the nearest prototype. Together they promote both diversity and discriminability — the right objectives for a prototype-based anomaly detector.

Test-Time Updation of memory items is a legitimate and pragmatic design choice. Anomaly detection systems are often deployed in environments where the definition of 'normal' drifts slightly over time or differs between scenes. Allowing the memory to adapt to normal observations seen at inference time (without any anomaly labels) is a principled form of transductive learning. The key constraint is that only confirmed-normal observations should update memory — since the system is trained on normal data and updates only when reconstruction quality is high, the implicit assumption is self-consistent.

The use of proxy tasks (frame reconstruction and future frame prediction) as the training signal is standard practice in video anomaly detection and is well-motivated: labels for anomalies are expensive and rare, but normal video is abundant. Training on prediction/reconstruction creates a rich supervisory signal without requiring anomaly annotations.

A likely critique will target the circularity of Test-Time Updation — if an anomalous frame is mistakenly incorporated into memory, the system degrades. The defense is that the update is gated on reconstruction quality: frames that reconstruct poorly (i.e., likely anomalies) are not incorporated. This is not perfectly circular because the reconstruction quality check precedes the memory update decision.

Another anticipated critique is that the hybrid score is a heuristic combination requiring manual weighting. This is partially valid — the weighting is dataset-specific — but the design accepts this as a tuning parameter rather than a fundamental flaw. The alternative (a learned score combiner) would require labeled anomaly data, which violates the unsupervised constraint. The heuristic combination is therefore the appropriate choice given the problem constraints.
all_issues_raised: Memory size is a critical free parameter that is neither theoretically justified nor ablated — too small degrades normal reconstruction, too large allows anomaly memorization, and the optimal range is dataset-specific without principled selection.

Test-Time Updation during inference creates ordering-dependent evaluation: AUC-ROC computed over a sequence of adaptively updated model states is not a valid i.i.d. statistic, and reported metrics are unstable with respect to test-set presentation order.

The hybrid Abnormality Score combines PSNR (pixel space) with L2-to-memory (feature space) using an unvalidated weighting — the two signals are not on commensurate scales, and the reported result reflects one unanalyzed combination.

Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss are in direct tension with each other and with memory size, creating a three-way hyperparameter interaction that is never ablated, making it impossible to determine whether the loss design or specific settings drives reported gains.
all_issues_adjudicated: Memory size is a critical free parameter that is neither theoretically justified nor ablated — too small degrades normal reconstruction, too large allows anomaly memorization, and the optimal range is dataset-specific without principled selection.

Test-Time Updation during inference creates ordering-dependent evaluation: AUC-ROC computed over a sequence of adaptively updated model states is not a valid i.i.d. statistic, and reported metrics are unstable with respect to test-set presentation order.

The hybrid Abnormality Score combines PSNR (pixel space) with L2-to-memory (feature space) using an unvalidated weighting — the two signals are not on commensurate scales, and the reported result reflects one unanalyzed combination.

Separateness Loss and Compactness Loss are in direct tension with each other and with memory size, creating a three-way hyperparameter interaction that is never ablated, making it impossible to determine whether the loss design or specific settings drives reported gains.
